channel.title: NYT > Science
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: How to Catch a Meteor Shower From Halley’s Comet
item.description: Known for their speed, the Eta Aquarids will reach their peak on Tuesday night into Wednesday morning.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:description: Halley’s comet over Easter Island in 1986. The Eta Aquarids meteor shower is the result of debris from Halley’s tail.

item.title: Trump Administration Orders Rapid End to Hunting Regulations on Federal Lands
item.description: Internal Interior Department documents show parks are imposing sweeping changes like expanding areas where firearms can be used and allowing game to be cleaned in restrooms.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.category: Jean Lafitte National Historical Park and Preserve (La)
item.media:description: Curecanti National Recreation Area in Colorado, where a prohibition on discharging firearms around trails is one of dozens of hunting regulations that will be taken off the books.

item.title: A Long, Strange Trip: How the G.O.P. Came to Embrace Psychedelic Drugs
item.description: For decades, conservatives were adamantly opposed to the use of drugs like psilocybin and LSD. Now, the Trump administration has made a sharp pivot.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Jacobs
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump patted the hand of W. Bryan Hubbard, founder of the advocacy group Americans for Ibogaine, as the podcaster Joe Rogan looked on in an Oval Office ceremony this month.

item.title: Shipwreck Reveals Fate of Vanished World War I Coast Guard Cutter
item.description: The Tampa disappeared in 1918 with 131 British and American personnel and civilians aboard. It was the largest single American naval combat loss of life in World War I
item.dc:creator: Christine Hauser
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: An original photo print of the Lac La Belle docked at Marquette, Mich., in 1866.

item.title: Top Psychiatrists Call for a Greater Focus on Ceasing Medication
item.description: With Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. aiming to rein in the use of psychiatric drugs, psychiatrists are preemptively addressing how and when patients should quit taking them.
item.dc:creator: Ellen Barry

item.title: What’s Safe to Eat? Birds of a Feather Learn Together
item.description: In a study, Australian cockatoos figured out that a new food was OK to consume by observing one another, a vivid example of “social learning” in animals.
item.dc:creator: Kate Golembiewski

item.title: In Permissive Amsterdam, Ads for Fossil Fuels or Meat Are Now Verboden
item.description: The Dutch city has outlawed advertising that promotes lifestyles linked to high carbon emissions, which is a driver of climate change. It’s a first for a world capital.
item.dc:creator: Cara Buckley

item.title: J. Craig Venter, Scientist Who Decoded the Human Genome, Dies at 79
item.description: A risk-taking outsider, he brought speed, competition and controversy to one of science’s biggest races.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Wade
item.media:credit: Sandy Huffaker for The New York Times
item.media:description: J. Craig Venter in 2010 in his San Diego greenhouse, where algae was being studied for its potential as a motor fuel.

item.title: The Murky Ethics of Swimming With Killer Whales
item.description: Only two places in the world allow tourists to enter the water with the ocean’s apex predator. But the safety of both species is a growing concern.
item.dc:creator: Alexa Robles-Gil and Meghan Dhaliwal
item.media:credit: Meghan Dhaliwal for The New York Times

item.title: How Ancient Centipede Ancestors Conquered the Earth
item.description: A long-neglected fossil seems to show the evolutionary leap that let the ancestors of today’s many-legged arthropods crawl forth from the seas.
item.dc:creator: Jack Tamisiea
item.category: Proceedings of the Royal Society B (Journal)

item.title: Hantavirus Outbreaks Are Rare, but They Aren’t Going Away and There’s No Cure
item.description: Since the family of rodent-borne infections were identified in the 1950s, they have turned up all over the world.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:description: A cruise ship remained stranded off the coast of Cape Verde after a suspected hantavirus outbreak on the vessel killed at least three people and left several others ill.

item.title: F.D.A. Blocked Publication of Research Finding Covid and Shingles Vaccines Were Safe
item.description: The agency’s scientists and data contractors reviewed millions of patient records for studies that were pulled back before release.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Kim Raff for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Covid-19 vaccine distribution site in Salt Lake City in 2022.

item.title: The Uncertain Fate of France’s Last Two Captive Orcas
item.description: France must decide what to do with two orcas marooned at a deserted marine park: move them to another park, or release them into an open-water sanctuary.
item.dc:creator: Ana Castelain
item.media:description: Keijo, an orca, in November at the Marineland park in France. Because he and his mother were born in captivity, experts say they would struggle in the open sea.

item.title: Kennedy Starts a Push to Help Americans Quit Antidepressants
item.description: The health secretary has long complained that Americans overuse psychiatric medications. New policies he is introducing aim to change that.
item.dc:creator: Ellen Barry
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Monday’s announcements were Health Secretary Kennedy’s first significant step to reduce the use of antidepressants.

item.title: More Than 150 Wind Projects Stall as Pentagon Delays Reviews
item.description: The delays, which companies say have worsened significantly in recent weeks, are the latest step in the Trump administration’s efforts to block wind power.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: A construction barge being loaded with wind turbine components for construction of a wind farm in Portsmouth, Va., earlier this year.

item.title: In Venice, an Ocean-Inspired Exhibition Takes Visitors Under the Sea
item.description: Inside a former convent on an island, immersive artworks invite visitors to “stretch their sense of self to include the vastness of the ocean.”
item.dc:creator: Keridwen Cornelius

item.title: Trump Administration Sues Minnesota to Block Climate Lawsuit
item.description: The Justice Department is seeking to block a lawsuit filed against major players in the fossil fuel industry over their role in climate change.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:description: Minnesota’s attorney general, Keith Ellison, filed Minnesota’s lawsuit against oil interests six years ago and was named as a defendant in the Trump administration’s lawsuit filed on Monday.

item.title: What to Know About Orphines, a New Class of Deadly Opioids
item.description: The drugs are 10 times more dangerous than fentanyl. They are showing up in street drugs in the South and the Midwest, and will most likely spread to other regions.
item.dc:creator: Jan Hoffman
item.media:credit: Brett Carlsen for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Knoxville, Tenn., police officer at the scene of a suspected overdose last month. The greater Knoxville area has seen a startling spike in overdose deaths driven by the presence of orphines.

item.title: A Medical Examiner Chases Down an Elusive Killer
item.description: Unusual opioids are infiltrating street drugs. Knoxville’s top forensic doctor is on the front lines, pressing to quickly identify the most lethal.
item.dc:creator: Jan Hoffman and Brett Carlsen
item.media:credit: Brett Carlsen for The New York Times

item.title: 3 Medical Routines That Older People May Not Need
item.description: Some screenings and treatments no longer make sense for patients as they age. Researchers have just added a few more to the list.
item.dc:creator: Paula Span

item.title: FEMA Disaster Aid is Flowing Slowly In Trump’s Second Term
item.description: During President Trump’s second term, the disaster declarations that unlock money are taking longer than in the past. Blue states wait the longest and they hear ‘no’ more often.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance and Ashley Cai
item.media:description: A damaged home in Enid, Okla., on April 24, after a tornado ripped through the night before.

item.title: How Running Shoes Have Evolved, From Ancient Greece to a Record-Breaking Marathon Time
item.description: The race to near-weightlessness has been a driving force of innovation in running sneakers and helped lead to records shattering at the London Marathon.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan

item.title: FEMA Is Reversing Job Cuts Made Under Kristi Noem
item.description: The agency said staff members who had been let go or placed on administrative leave were now needed to prepare for hurricanes and the World Cup.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:description: Former Homeland Security Kristi Noem during a visit to FEMA headquarters in Washington in January.

item.title: Chonkers the ‘Food-Motivated’ Sea Lion Plops Into San Francisco
item.description: Wildlife experts have been tracking the Steller sea lion since he appeared last month at a popular tourist spot near the end of Fisherman’s Wharf.
item.dc:creator: Livia Albeck-Ripka

item.title: Smog in Phoenix and Salt Lake City? The E.P.A. Is Blaming Asia.
item.description: The Trump administration says the cities shouldn’t be penalized for unhealthy air because pollution can blow in from abroad. Some experts say that’s preposterous.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: Phoenix has long struggled with smog collecting in its bowl-shaped topography. The city failed to meet a federal air pollution standard in 2024.

item.title: The Vaccine Skeptic in Trump’s New C.D.C. Leadership Team
item.description: Dr. Sara Brenner is a physician, an F.D.A. official and a “MAHA mom” who has said people should not reflexively believe in the benefits of vaccines.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: Dr. Sara Brenner fully embraced the Make America Healthy Again movement after being named the acting commissioner of the Food and Drug Administration, signing her first emails to the agency’s staff with the slogan.

item.title: Forest Service Research Labs Are Closing
item.description: A research lab in Washington State tracks ecological changes in a warming climate and provides scientific guidance for forest managers. It is one of 57 such facilities being shuttered.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler and John Stember
item.media:credit: John Stember for The New York Times

item.title: Peter Raven Dies at 89; Botanist Warned of Perils of Climate Change
item.description: He transformed the Missouri Botanical Garden into an international research hub and laid out the existential threats posed by deforestation and unchecked development.
item.dc:creator: Keith Schneider
item.media:credit: Peter Morris/Fairfax Media, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Dr. Peter Raven in 1990 with a display of butterfly specimens at the Missouri Botanical Garden in St. Louis, where he was the director. An ardent conservationist, he once said, “As we destroy the environment, we are destroying ourselves.”

item.title: How to Build a Better Kind of Nuclear Power? This Side Hustle Might Help.
item.description: Zap Energy says its ultimate goal is safe, clean energy from fusion. To help get there, it’s starting to build fission reactors.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:credit: Grant Hindsley for The New York Times
item.media:description: Benj Conway, Zap’s president and co-founder.

item.title: Scientists Unveil ‘Long Lost’ Map for Smell
item.description: The odor receptors in the nose are not distributed at random but organized in a precise spatial pattern, two new studies reveal.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes

item.title: A.I. Bots Told Scientists How to Make Biological Weapons
item.description: Scientists shared transcripts with The Times in which chatbots described how to assemble deadly pathogens and unleash them in public spaces.
item.dc:creator: Gabriel J.X. Dance
